question: Which of the following is correct regarding Java identifiers?
explanation: Java identifiers can start with a letter, an underscore (_), or a dollar sign ($), but cannot start with a digit. Also, Java reserved words (such as class, int, if, etc.) cannot be used as identifiers. A single underscore (_) became a reserved word starting from Java 9.

choices.text: Identifiers can start with a digit
A single underscore (_) can be used as an identifier
An identifier starting with a dollar sign ($) is valid
A hyphen (-) can be included in an identifier

question: Which of the following is correct regarding the javac and java commands?
explanation: The javac command compiles Java source files (.java) into bytecode (.class). The java command executes bytecode on the JVM. Since Java SE 11, the java command also has the ability to directly execute a single source file, but the javac command has not been deprecated.

choices.text: The javac command is used to execute bytecode
The java command is used to compile .java files
The file extension generated by the javac command is .class
There is no way to run a Java program without the javac command

question: What is the output when this code is executed?
explanation: In the first line, 5 + 3 = 8 is calculated first, resulting in "8 items". In the second line, since "Total: " is a string, all subsequent + operators become string concatenation, resulting in "Total: 53". When using the + operator with strings and numbers, the left-to-right evaluation order is important.

choices.text:
8 items
Total: 8

8 items
Total: 53

53 items
Total: 53
A compilation error occurs

question: This code has a compilation error. Which of the following is the cause?
explanation: Only one public class can be defined in a single source file. Defining two public classes, public class Greeting and public class Farewell, in the file Greeting.java results in a compilation error. Multiple non-public classes can be defined in the same file.

choices.text: Because two main methods are defined
Because there are two public classes in a single source file
Because the class names are different
Because the arguments of System.out.println are different

explanation: The package keyword is used for package declarations. A package declaration is placed at the beginning of a source file (excluding comments) and specifies the package to which the class belongs. By convention, package names use reverse domain names.

choices.text: package
import
module
namespace

question: Which of the following is correct regarding Java primitive types?
explanation: Java has 8 primitive types: byte (8-bit), short (16-bit), int (32-bit), long (64-bit), float (32-bit), double (64-bit), char (16-bit), and boolean. char is an unsigned 16-bit integer that represents Unicode characters. short is a 16-bit signed integer type.

choices.text: char is an 8-bit signed integer type
long is a 32-bit integer type
float is a 64-bit floating-point type
short is a 16-bit signed integer type

question: What is the output when this code is executed?
explanation: The maximum value of byte is 127. Incrementing with b++ should make it 128, but since it exceeds the byte range (-128 to 127), an overflow occurs and the value wraps around to -128. In Java, integer overflow of primitive types does not throw an exception; the bit representation wraps around.

choices.text: A compilation error occurs

question: This code has a compilation error. Which of the following is the cause?
explanation: When using var (local variable type inference), an initializer is required at the time of declaration. Since the compiler infers the type from the initializer, declaring var price; without initialization results in a compilation error. Writing var price = 1500; would correctly infer it as int type.

choices.text: Because var cannot be used in Java SE 17
Because a variable declared with var requires an initializer, which is missing
Because the variable name price is a reserved word
Because var cannot infer the int type

question:
Fill in the blank to correctly declare a long variable.

long population = 8000000000___;
explanation: A long literal requires the suffix L or l. Since 8000000000 exceeds the int range (-2147483648 to 2147483647), omitting the L suffix results in a compilation error. Using the uppercase L is recommended for readability.

choices.text: L
D
F
Nothing is needed

question: Which of the following is correct regarding Java type conversion?
explanation: In Java, conversion from a smaller type to a larger type (widening conversion) is performed implicitly. For example, int to long is automatically converted. On the other hand, conversion from a larger type to a smaller type (narrowing conversion) requires an explicit cast. Conversion between boolean and other primitive types is not possible.

choices.text: Converting from int to double requires a cast
Converting from double to int is performed implicitly
Converting from long to int requires a cast
Converting from boolean to int is possible

question: Which of the following is the correct value of the variable result in this code?
explanation: % is the remainder operator, which returns the remainder of dividing a by b. Since 17 ÷ 5 = 3 remainder 2, 17 % 5 equals 2. The remainder operator can be used with both integer and floating-point types, and the sign of the result matches the sign of the dividend.

question: What is the output when this code is executed?
explanation: x++ is a post-increment, which uses the current value of 10 in the expression and then increments x to 11. ++x is a pre-increment, which increments x to 12 and then uses that value. Therefore, y = 10 + 12 = 22. The final values are x = 12 and y = 22.

question: This code has a compilation error. Which of the following is the cause?
explanation: The bitwise AND operator (&) cannot be used between boolean and int types. The & operator can be used between two booleans or two ints, but using it with mismatched types results in a compilation error. boolean & boolean and int & int are valid.

choices.text: Because the & operator cannot be used in Java
Because the & operator is used between boolean and int types
Because flag is not declared as final
Because the result is received as int type

question: Select all correct statements about Java operators. (Choose 2)
explanation: && is the short-circuit logical AND operator, which does not evaluate the right operand if the left operand is false. The ternary operator (condition ? value1 : value2) is the only ternary operator that returns a value based on the result of the condition expression. The & operator is a non-short-circuit operator that always evaluates both operands and is also used for bitwise operations.

choices.text: The && operator does not evaluate the right operand if the left operand is false
The || operator evaluates the right operand even if the left operand is true
The ternary operator can return a value
The == operator compares the contents of Strings

question: Which of the following is the correct output of this code?
explanation: && performs short-circuit evaluation. Since num != 0 is false, the right operand 10 / num is not evaluated. If & (non-short-circuit) were used instead, an ArithmeticException would occur due to division by zero. However, with &&, if the left operand is false, the right operand is not evaluated, so no exception occurs and false is printed.

choices.text: true
false
An ArithmeticException is thrown
A compilation error occurs

question: Which of the following is correct regarding Java SE 17 switch expressions?
explanation: In switch expressions, all possible values must be covered, and if they are not, a default is required. Also, when executing multiple statements in a switch expression, a block ({}) is used and the yield keyword is used to return a value. With arrow syntax (->), fall-through does not occur.

choices.text: The default clause can always be omitted in switch expressions
Fall-through also occurs with arrow syntax (->) in switch expressions
The yield keyword is used to return a value from a block in a switch expression
String type cannot be used in switch expressions

question: What is the output when this code is executed?
explanation: When an if statement has no braces, only the immediately following single statement becomes the body of the if statement. System.out.println("水分補給") is unrelated to the if statement and is always executed. Since temperature > 30 is false, "暑い" is not printed, but "水分補給" is always printed. Indentation does not affect the Java compiler.

choices.text:
暑い
水分補給
水分補給
Nothing is printed
A compilation error occurs

question: This code has a compilation error. Which of the following is the cause?
explanation: The long type cannot be used as the argument of a switch statement. The types allowed in a switch statement are byte, short, char, int (and their wrapper types), String, and enum types. long, float, double, and boolean types cannot be used.

choices.text: Because case 100 uses a colon instead of a semicolon
Because long type cannot be used in a switch statement
Because there is no break statement in the default block
Because the case value is missing the L suffix

explanation: When using a block ({}) with arrow syntax in a switch expression, the yield keyword is used to return a value. Note that yield is used instead of return. yield is a keyword introduced in Java SE 14, specifically for returning values from switch expressions.

choices.text: yield
return
break
output

question: Which of the following is correct regarding switch expression syntax?
explanation: With arrow syntax (->) in switch expressions, multiple labels can be specified for a single case separated by commas (e.g., case 1, 2, 3 -> "value"). Also, switch expressions must cover all cases or include a default case. Arrow syntax and colon syntax cannot be mixed within the same switch.

choices.text: case 1, 2, 3 -> "value"; is a valid expression
null can be specified as a case value in switch expressions
Arrow syntax (->) and colon syntax (:) can be mixed
A switch expression can be used as the return value of a void method

question: Which of the following is correct for-loop syntax?
explanation: All three parts of a for statement — the initialization, condition, and update — can be omitted. for(;;) creates an infinite loop. In the initialization part, multiple variables of the same type can be declared separated by commas, and multiple expressions can also be written separated by commas in the update part. However, variables of different types cannot be declared in the initialization part.

choices.text: for(int i=0, long j=0; i<10; i++) {}
for(int i=0; i<10) {}
for(i=0; i<10; i++) {} (where i is undeclared)

question: What is the output when this code is executed?
explanation: The for loop iterates from 0 to 4, but when i == 3, the continue statement is executed, which skips the remaining processing of that iteration. Therefore, 3 is not printed, and 0, 1, 2, 4 are printed separated by spaces. continue advances to the next iteration of the loop.

question: What is the output when this code is executed?
explanation: A do-while loop evaluates the condition after executing the loop body, so the loop body is executed at least once. With count = 10, the loop body executes and prints "10 ". Then count is incremented to 11 by count++, and since the while condition count < 10 is false, the loop exits.

choices.text: Nothing is printed
An infinite loop occurs

question: This code has a compilation error. Which of the following is the cause?
explanation: The label inner referenced in break inner; is not defined. To use a label, the target loop must be labeled. In this example, either the inner label must be added to the inner for loop (inner:), or the label in the break statement must be changed to the already defined outer.

choices.text: Because the label outer is not used
Because the label inner is not defined
Because break statements cannot be used in nested for loops
Because labels do not need a colon

question: Which of the following is correct regarding the difference between while and do-while loops?
explanation: A while loop evaluates the condition first, so if the condition is false, the loop body is never executed. On the other hand, a do-while loop executes the loop body first and then evaluates the condition, so the loop body is executed at least once. Also, a semicolon is required at the end of a do-while statement.

choices.text: A while loop executes the loop body at least once
A do-while loop executes at least once even if the condition is false
while and do-while loops behave exactly the same
The break statement cannot be used in a do-while loop

question: Which of the following is a correct array declaration?
explanation: There are multiple ways to declare arrays in Java. Both int[] arr and int arr[] are valid, but int[] arr is recommended. Array literal initialization ({1,2,3}) can only be used at the time of declaration without new. The form new int[]{} where the size is not specified but initial values are provided is also valid.

choices.text: int[] arr = new int[];
int[] arr = new int[3]{1, 2, 3};

question: What is the output when this code is executed?
explanation: Arrays.copyOf creates a new array of the specified length from the original array. Since 3 is specified as the second argument, the first 3 elements from the original array are copied. If a shorter length than the original array is specified, the array is truncated. If a longer length is specified, the remaining elements are filled with the default value (0 for int).

choices.text: A compilation error occurs

question: What happens when this code is executed?
explanation: The array size is 3 (indices 0 to 2), but data[3] is outside the index range. This code compiles successfully, but an ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException is thrown at runtime. Array indices range from 0 to length-1.

choices.text: A compilation error occurs
An ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException occurs at runtime
0 is assigned to data[3]
The array is automatically expanded

question: Select all correct statements about arrays. (Select 2)
explanation: Arrays are objects and are stored in heap memory. The size of an array cannot be changed after creation. Java arrays are fixed-length, and if a variable-length array is needed, collection classes such as ArrayList should be used. Arrays inherit from the java.lang.Object class.

choices.text: The size of an array cannot be changed after creation
Arrays are primitive types
Arrays inherit from the Object class
Array elements can contain a mix of different types

question: Which of the following is correct about multidimensional arrays?
code: int[][] matrix = new int[3][];
explanation: Java's multidimensional arrays support jagged arrays (arrays with rows of different lengths). int[][] matrix = new int[3][]; creates an array where the first dimension has a size of 3, and the second dimension is unspecified. Each row can be individually initialized with a different size. matrix[0] is null before initialization.

choices.text: This code causes a compilation error
matrix[0] is null
matrix[0] is an empty array (length 0)
All dimensions must be specified at the same time

question: Which of the following is correct about method varargs (variable-length arguments)?
explanation: Varargs are declared by writing ... after the type name (e.g., int... numbers). A varargs parameter can only be specified once, as the last parameter in a method's parameter list. Inside the method, it is treated as an array. It is also possible to pass zero arguments.

choices.text: A varargs parameter can be placed at any position in the parameter list
A method can have multiple varargs parameters
A varargs parameter can only be specified once, as the last parameter in the parameter list
At least one argument must be passed to a varargs parameter

question: What is the output when this code is executed?
explanation: Java uses pass-by-value for everything. Since arrays are reference types, a copy of the reference is passed. arr[0] = 99 modifies the element of the original array. On the other hand, val is a copy of the primitive type int, so assigning val = 99 does not affect x in the calling method. The result is 99, 10.

question: This code has a compilation error. Which of the following is the cause?
explanation: Method overloading requires differences in the type, number, or order of parameters. Methods that differ only in return type are not considered valid overloads and cause a compilation error. In this example, both calc methods take (int, int) parameters and differ only in return type.

choices.text: Because static methods cannot be overloaded
Because methods that differ only in return type are not valid overloads
Because the method name calc is a reserved word
Because the type is not specified when calling from the main method

question: Which of the following is correct about how method arguments are passed in Java?
explanation: In Java, all arguments are passed by value. For primitive types, a copy of the value is passed. For reference types, a copy of the reference to the object is passed. Since it is a copy of the reference, the contents of the referenced object can be modified, but reassigning the reference to a different object does not affect the caller.

choices.text: Primitive types are passed by reference, and object types are passed by value
All arguments are passed by value
Object types are passed by reference
final parameters are passed by reference

explanation: To declare varargs, write ... (three dots) after the type name. By using double... values, the method can accept zero or more double values as an array. Inside the method, values is treated as double[].

question: Which of the following is correct about StringBuilder?
explanation: StringBuilder is a mutable string class. Unlike String, appending or modifying strings does not create a new object. StringBuilder is not thread-safe, but it is faster than StringBuffer for single-threaded string operations.

choices.text: StringBuilder is immutable
StringBuilder is thread-safe
The append method of StringBuilder returns a new object
StringBuilder performs string modifications on the same object

question: What is the output when this code is executed?
explanation: substring(5, 8) returns the substring from index 5 to 7 (8 is exclusive). In "Java Programming", index 5 is 'P', 6 is 'r', and 7 is 'o', so "Pro" is returned. indexOf('a') returns the index of the first occurrence of 'a', which is at index 1.

choices.text:
Pro
1

Prog
1

Pro
3

rog
1

question: What is the output when this code is executed?
explanation: Text blocks (""") were introduced in Java SE 15. Searching for "Java" using contains on the string inside the text block returns true. Text blocks remove incidental whitespace, and after removing leading and trailing whitespace with strip(), the string starts with "{", so startsWith("{") also returns true.

choices.text:
true
true

true
false

false
true
A compilation error occurs

question: Which of the following is correct about the syntax of text blocks (""")?
explanation: A line break is required after the opening delimiter (""") of a text block. Content cannot be written on the same line as the opening """. The closing """ can be written on the same line as the content. Line breaks within the text block are included as-is, and incidental whitespace is removed by the compiler.

choices.text: Content can be written immediately after the opening """
A line break is required after the opening """
The closing """ must be written on a separate line
Line breaks within a text block must be written as \n

question: Which of the following is correct about String class methods?
explanation: String is an immutable class. Methods such as replace, toUpperCase, and trim do not modify the original string but return a new String object. String.strip() was added in Java SE 11 and removes leading and trailing whitespace with Unicode support. trim() removes only ASCII whitespace.

choices.text: The replace method modifies the original String object
The contents of a String object cannot be changed after creation
trim() and strip() behave exactly the same way
length is a field, not a method

question: Which of the following is correct about Java records?
explanation: A record class is implicitly final and cannot extend other classes (it implicitly extends java.lang.Record). However, it can implement interfaces. All fields of a record are final, and no setters are generated. Custom methods and static methods can be added.

choices.text: A record can extend other classes
Fields of a record are mutable
A record can implement interfaces
Custom methods cannot be added to a record

question: What is the output when this code is executed?
explanation: Records are immutable data classes introduced in Java SE 16. A record automatically generates a constructor, accessor methods (methods with the same name as the field), equals, hashCode, and toString. The accessor methods use the field name itself (name(), price()) rather than getXxx().

choices.text:
Coffee
350
Product[name=Coffee, price=350]
A compilation error occurs

null
0

question: This code has a compilation error. Which of the following is the cause?
explanation: Since the Employee class has constructors with parameters defined, the default constructor (no-argument constructor) is not automatically generated by the compiler. Attempting to instantiate with new Employee() without arguments causes a compilation error. If a no-argument constructor is also needed, it must be explicitly defined.

choices.text: Because two constructors are defined
Because there is no no-argument constructor
Because the Main class is not public
Because the this keyword is used incorrectly

question: Select all correct statements about records. (Select 2)
explanation: A record is implicitly a final class and cannot extend other classes using the extends clause. In a record's compact constructor, parameter validation and other operations can be performed. Static methods and instance methods can be added to a record. A record automatically generates toString, equals, and hashCode methods.

choices.text: A record is implicitly final
A record can be abstract
A record automatically generates a toString method
Fields of a record are public

question: Which of the following is correct about Java constructors?
explanation: A constructor does not have a return type, including void. If a return type is specified, it is treated as a regular method, not a constructor. The constructor name must be the same as the class name. The default constructor is automatically generated by the compiler only when no constructors are defined.

choices.text: A constructor must have a void return type
The default constructor is automatically generated even if other constructors are defined
The constructor name must be the same as the class name
A constructor is defined as a static method

question: Which of the following is correct about access modifiers?
explanation: Java has four access modifiers: public (accessible from all classes), protected (accessible from the same package and subclasses), default (package-private, same package only), and private (same class only). In order from widest to narrowest access: public > protected > default > private.

choices.text: Members with the protected modifier can only be accessed from the same package
Omitting the access modifier is the same as public
private members can only be accessed from within the same class
public members can only be accessed from subclasses

question: What is the output when this code is executed?
explanation: The Counter class declares an encapsulated count field as private and provides public increment() and getCount() methods. Calling increment() three times sets count to 3. getCount() retrieves this value and prints it, so 3 is output.

choices.text: A compilation error occurs

question: This code has a compilation error. Which of the following is the cause?
explanation: Since the balance field is declared as private, it cannot be accessed directly from outside the Account class. Attempting to access acc.balance from the BankApp class causes a compilation error. A public getter method needs to be added to access it.

choices.text: Because the Account class has no getter method
Because a private field is being accessed from outside the class
Because the Account class is not public
Because fields of type double require the protected modifier

question: Which of the following is the most appropriate class design that correctly applies encapsulation?
explanation: In encapsulation, fields are declared as private and accessed through public getter/setter methods. This enables validation and control over field values. Declaring fields as private prevents direct access from external classes.

choices.text: Declare fields as private and provide public getters/setters
Declare fields as public and allow direct access
Declare fields as protected and allow access from the same package
Declare fields as static and access them by class name

question: Which of the following is NOT a correct condition for creating an immutable class?
explanation: To create an immutable class, make the class final, declare fields as private final, do not provide setters, and perform defensive copies for references to mutable objects. Making fields protected allows access from subclasses, which compromises immutability.

choices.text: Make the class final
Declare fields as private final
Do not provide setter methods
Declare fields as protected

question: Which of the following is correct regarding inheritance in Java?
explanation: Java does not allow multiple inheritance of classes. A class can only extend one class using the extends clause. However, multiple interface implementation (implementing multiple interfaces using the implements clause) is allowed. A final class cannot have subclasses.

choices.text: A class can inherit from multiple classes simultaneously
A final class cannot have subclasses
A subclass can directly access private members of the parent class
Java does not have a class inheritance feature

question: What is the output when this code is executed?
explanation: When a subclass constructor is called, super() is implicitly called first to execute the parent class constructor. Therefore, "P " is output first, and then the rest of the Child constructor executes to output "C ".

choices.text: C P
P C
P
C

question: The following code has a compilation error. Which of the following is the cause of the error?
explanation: Subclasses of a sealed class must specify one of the modifiers: final, sealed, or non-sealed. Since the Car class does not have any of these modifiers, it causes a compilation error. class Car must be changed to final class Car, sealed class Car, or non-sealed class Car.

choices.text: Because the sealed keyword is in the wrong position
Because the Car class is missing a final, sealed, or non-sealed modifier
Because only two classes are specified in permits
Because the Truck class is final and cannot extend Vehicle

question: Which of the following is correct regarding sealed classes?
explanation: Sealed classes were officially introduced in Java SE 17. A sealed class allows only the classes specified in the permits clause to be subclasses. Permitted subclasses must be declared as final, sealed, or non-sealed. A sealed class can also be declared as abstract.

choices.text: A sealed class cannot have subclasses
Subclasses of a sealed class must be final, sealed, or non-sealed
A sealed class cannot implement interfaces
A sealed class cannot be abstract

explanation: To call the parent class constructor from a subclass constructor, use super(). By writing super(label), the parameterized constructor of the parent class Base is called. super() must be the first statement in the constructor.

choices.text: super
this
base
parent

question: Which of the following casts is performed correctly in this code?
explanation: A downcast is a conversion from a parent class type to a child class type, and requires an explicit cast. If a Fruit variable actually references an Apple object, it can be cast with (Apple). However, attempting to cast an Orange object to Apple will throw a ClassCastException.

choices.text: Fruit f = new Apple(); Apple a = (Apple) f;
Fruit f = new Orange(); Apple a = (Apple) f;
Apple a = new Apple(); Orange o = (Orange) a;
Object o = new Fruit(); String s = (String) o;

question: What is the output when this code is executed?
explanation: In the first check(-5), obj is of Integer type (autoboxing). obj instanceof Integer n is true, but n > 0 is false (-5 > 0 is false), so the entire if condition evaluates to false due to short-circuit evaluation with &&. The next else if checks for String type, which is also false, so "その他" (Other) is output. In the second check("ABC"), it is not Integer so if is false, but it is String so the else if is true, and "文字列: 3" (String: 3) is output.

choices.text:
正の整数: -5
文字列: 3

その他
文字列: 3
A compilation error occurs

正の整数: -5
その他

question: The following code has a compilation error. Which of the following is the cause of the error?
explanation: When overriding a method, the return type must be the same type or a covariant return type (subtype). The return type cannot be changed from void in the parent class to int in the subclass. Since void is not a primitive type, covariant return types do not apply.

choices.text: Because the @Override annotation is incorrect
Because the return type was changed from void to int in the override
Because the method name print is a reserved word
Because there is a return statement after System.out.println

question: Select all correct answers regarding polymorphism. (Choose 2)
explanation: With polymorphism, a parent class variable can reference a subclass object. Overridden method calls are resolved at runtime based on the actual type of the object (dynamic binding). However, static methods are not dynamically bound and are called based on the declared type.

choices.text: A subclass instance can be assigned to a parent class variable
Static methods are also dynamically bound through overriding
An overridden method is called based on the actual object type at runtime
A parent class instance can be directly assigned to a subclass variable

question: Which of the following is correct regarding pattern matching for instanceof?
explanation: With pattern matching for instanceof, type checking and variable assignment can be done in a single expression. The scope of a pattern variable is valid only where the variable has definitely passed the type check. For example, a variable declared with instanceof String in an if condition can be used within the then block.

choices.text: A pattern variable can always be used outside the if statement
A pattern variable declared with the instanceof operator can be accessed without casting
Pattern matching can only be used in switch expressions
A pattern variable must be declared as final

question: Which of the following is correct regarding default methods in interfaces?
explanation: Since Java SE 8, default methods can be defined in interfaces. Default methods have a body, and they do not cause a compilation error even if the implementing class does not override them. If multiple interfaces have default methods with the same name, the implementing class must override to resolve the conflict.

choices.text: A default method must always be overridden by the implementing class
A default method can have a body
Only one default method can be defined in an interface
A default method can be declared as private

question: What is the output when this code is executed?
explanation: The JapaneseGreeter class overrides the default method greet() of the Greetable interface. Although the variable g is of type Greetable, the actual object is a JapaneseGreeter, so the overridden "こんにちは" is output. Default methods can be overridden by implementing classes.

choices.text: Hello
こんにちは
A compilation error occurs
null

question: The following code has a compilation error. Which of the following is the cause of the error?
explanation: Subtypes (implementing classes or sub-interfaces) of a sealed interface must be final, sealed, or non-sealed. Since the Circle class does not have any of these modifiers, it causes a compilation error. It must be changed to final class Circle or non-sealed class Circle.

choices.text: Because sealed cannot be applied to an interface
Because the Circle class is missing a final, sealed, or non-sealed modifier
Because the Square class has a draw method definition
Because too few classes are specified in permits

question: Select all correct answers for members that can be defined in an interface. (Choose 3)
explanation: Since Java SE 8, interfaces can define abstract methods, default methods, and static methods. Since Java SE 9, private methods can also be defined. Instance fields cannot be defined (only constants: public static final). Constructors cannot be defined in interfaces.

choices.text: Abstract methods
Default methods
Instance fields
Static methods
Constructors

question: Which of the following is correct regarding variables in interfaces?
explanation: Fields declared in an interface are implicitly public static final. Even if the modifiers are omitted, these modifiers are automatically applied. Therefore, interface fields are constants, and initialization is required.

choices.text: Interface fields can be declared as private
Interface fields are instance variables
Interface fields are implicitly public static final
Fields cannot be declared in an interface

question: Which of the following is correct regarding checked and unchecked exceptions?
explanation: Checked exceptions are subclasses of Exception (excluding RuntimeException) and are checked at compile time. If a method may throw a checked exception, it must be handled with try-catch or declared with a throws clause. Unchecked exceptions are subclasses of RuntimeException and are not checked at compile time.

choices.text: NullPointerException is a checked exception
IOException is an unchecked exception
Checked exceptions must be handled with catch or throws
All exceptions must be caught

question: What is the output when this code is executed?
explanation: After "A " is output in the try block, an ArithmeticException occurs at 10/0. "B " is not executed. "C " is output in the catch block, and the finally block always executes regardless of whether an exception occurred, so "D " is output. The result is "A C D ".

choices.text: A B C D
A C D
A C
A D

question: The following code has a compilation error. Which of the following is the cause of the error?
explanation: In a multi-catch, the specified exception classes must not have an inheritance relationship. Since NullPointerException is a subclass of RuntimeException, specifying both in the same catch clause causes a compilation error. The subclass exception is already caught by the parent class exception, making it a redundant specification.

choices.text: Because multi-catch cannot specify more than two exceptions
Because NullPointerException and RuntimeException have an inheritance relationship
Because the variable e is not final
Because NullPointerException cannot be caught

question: Which of the following is correct regarding try-with-resources statements?
explanation: The try-with-resources statement automatically closes resources that implement the AutoCloseable interface. After the try block executes, the close() method of resources is called before catch and finally. Multiple resources can be declared separated by semicolons, and they are closed in reverse order of declaration.

choices.text: Resources are closed after the finally block
Resources used in try-with-resources only need to implement Closeable
Resources are closed in the reverse order of their declaration
A catch clause cannot be used with try-with-resources statements

explanation: To create a custom exception class, extend the Exception class (for checked exceptions) or RuntimeException class (for unchecked exceptions) using the extends keyword. The extends keyword defines the class inheritance relationship.

choices.text: extends
implements
throws
catches

question: Which of the following is correct regarding var?
explanation: var is a local variable type inference mechanism introduced in Java SE 10. var can only be used for local variable declarations and cannot be used for fields, method parameters, or return types. var is not a reserved word but a "reserved type name," so it can be used as a variable name or method name.

choices.text: var can be used for field type declarations
var can only be used for local variable declarations
var can be used as a method return type
var was deprecated in Java SE 17

question: What is the output when this code is executed?
explanation: When using var, the compiler infers the type from the right-hand side expression. List.of(1, 2, 3) returns an immutable List, so list is inferred as List<Integer>. list.size() returns 3, and size is inferred as int. The output is 3.

choices.text: A compilation error occurs
A ClassCastException occurs at runtime

question: The following code has a compilation error. Which of the following is the cause of the error?
explanation: var can only be used for local variable type inference. It cannot be used for instance fields, static fields, method parameters, or return types. In this example, var is used for an instance field, which causes a compilation error.

choices.text: Because the initial value 10 cannot be assigned with var
Because var cannot be used for instance fields
Because the field cannot be accessed from the main method
Because var was deprecated in Java SE 17

question: Which of the following uses of var does NOT cause a compilation error?
explanation: var message = "Hello"; is valid because the local variable is declared and initialized simultaneously, allowing the compiler to infer the String type. var x; causes an error because there is no initializer, var arr = {1,2,3}; causes an error because the type cannot be inferred from an array initializer, and var n = null; causes an error because the type cannot be inferred from null.

choices.text: var x;
var message = "Hello";
var arr = {1, 2, 3};
var n = null;

explanation: Since Java SE 10 (JEP 286), var can also be used for loop variables in for-each statements. The compiler infers the type of item as String from the type of items (List<String>). Using var in for-each statements allows omitting the type name.

choices.text: var
auto
Object
def

question: Which of the following is correct regarding the arrow syntax (->) in switch expressions?
explanation: In the arrow syntax of switch expressions, fall-through does not occur. Each case arm is independent, and a break statement is not needed. Multiple labels can be specified for a single case separated by commas (e.g., case 1, 2, 3 ->). When using a block ({}), yield is used to return a value. Arrow syntax can also be used in switch statements.

choices.text: A break statement is still required with arrow syntax
Fall-through does not occur with arrow syntax
Only one label can be specified per case with arrow syntax
Arrow syntax can only be used in switch expressions, not switch statements

question: What is the output when this code is executed?
explanation: In the switch expression, season matches "SUMMER". Inside the arrow syntax block, System.out.print("暑い！ ") is executed, then yield 92; returns the value 92. After that, System.out.println(numDays) outputs 92. The result is "暑い！ 92".

choices.text: 暑い！ 92

暑い！ 
92
A compilation error occurs

question: The following code has a compilation error. Which of the following is the cause of the error?
explanation: Arrow syntax (->) and colon syntax (:) cannot be mixed within the same switch expression (or switch statement). case 1 uses arrow syntax while case 2 uses colon syntax, which causes a compilation error. All cases must use the same syntax.

choices.text: Because the yield keyword is used incorrectly
Because arrow syntax (->) and colon syntax (:) are mixed
Because the default case value is 0
Because a switch expression cannot return an int type

question: Select all correct answers regarding switch expressions. (Choose 2)
explanation: Switch expressions were officially introduced in Java SE 14. Switch expressions return a value, so they can be assigned to variables or used as arguments. In switch expressions, all cases must return a value and must be exhaustive. With arrow syntax (->), there is no fall-through. If all values of an enum type are covered, default can be omitted.

choices.text: A switch expression can return a value that can be assigned to a variable
Enum types cannot be used in switch expressions
Fall-through does not occur with arrow syntax in switch expressions
A default case is always required in switch expressions

explanation: In the arrow syntax of switch expressions, -> is used after the case label to specify the value. Arrow syntax was introduced in Java SE 14, providing a simple notation without fall-through. The right-hand side of -> in each case directly becomes the value of the switch expression.

question: Which of the following is correct regarding module-info.java in the Java Module System?
explanation: module-info.java is the module declaration file and is placed in the default package (directly under the source root). The file contains module declarations and directives (requires, exports, opens, etc.). It cannot contain package declarations or import statements like regular Java classes.

choices.text: A package declaration cannot be written in module-info.java
Regular class definitions can also be written in module-info.java
Multiple module-info.java files can be placed in a single project
The filename module-info.java can be changed

question: Which of the following is correct regarding the difference between requires transitive and requires?
explanation: Because moduleA declares requires transitive moduleB, moduleC, which depends on moduleA, can automatically access the exported packages of moduleB as well. With a regular requires (without transitive), moduleC would not be able to directly access moduleB.

choices.text: moduleC can access the exported packages of moduleB without an explicit requires
moduleC needs a requires moduleB declaration to access moduleB
requires transitive is effective only at compile time and not at runtime
Using requires transitive exposes all packages of moduleB

question: The following module-info.java has a compilation error. Which of the following is the cause?
explanation: Duplicate requires directives for the same module are not allowed. Since the requires declaration for com.example.lib is written twice, it causes a compilation error.

choices.text: Because there is a duplicate requires for the same module
Because the transitive keyword is required for requires
Because there is no exports directive
Because the module name contains dots (.)

question: Which of the following is correct regarding automatic modules?
explanation: An automatic module is a module that is automatically generated when a JAR file without module-info.java is placed on the module path. The module name is automatically derived from the JAR filename. An automatic module implicitly exports all packages and requires all named modules.

choices.text: A JAR without module-info.java becomes an automatic module when placed on the module path
An automatic module only exposes exported packages
An automatic module depends only on the java.base module
The module name for an automatic module is randomly generated

question: Select all correct answers for directives that can be used in module-info.java.
explanation: The directives that can be used in module-info.java are: requires (dependency module declaration), exports (package exposure), opens (reflection exposure), uses (service usage declaration), and provides (service provision declaration) — five in total. imports is a Java import statement and is not a module directive.

choices.text: requires
exports
imports
provides
